mechanism: Stone-on-stone percussion driven by tidal/wave energy
status: Fourth and final entry in meteorological/hydrological arc 249-252

cobble_geology.rock_types: Volcanic (basalt, andesite) and metamorphic (greenstone, quartzite, schist) from Cascade Range and Olympics
cobble_geology.why_no_sand: PNW lacks conditions for sandy beach formation: no large fine-quartz sediment supply, unsuitable wave climate for sorting without removal, hard volcanic/metamorphic source rock
cobble_geology.visual_character: Grey to dark grey stones, wet, gleaming, arranged by tidal high-water marks. Austere grey beach under grey sky.

acoustic_physics.primary_mechanism: Stone-on-stone impact: cobble against cobble when wave energy moves them

wave_cycle_sounds.approach: Surge over wet stones -- rushing, hissing, waterfall-like
wave_cycle_sounds.maximum_uprush: Stones in turbulent suspension -- high-amplitude complex percussion
wave_cycle_sounds.backwash: THE SIGNATURE SOUND: retreating water drags cobbles downslope. Sustained clatter of thousands of stones sliding against each other.
wave_cycle_sounds.pause: Individual stone-to-stone contacts as beach settles. Silence before next wave.

acoustic_physics.frequency_character: Click-clatter (moderate conditions); grinding (storm surge / large cobble). Lower frequency than rain; more rhythmically structured than wind.
acoustic_physics.lithophone_concept: Natural lithophone: wave energy = player; cobbles = instrument; beach geometry = rhythm. PNW coast as great lithophonic landscape.
acoustic_physics.e4_placement: Present, substantial, audible 100-200m. More dynamic and rhythmically complex than rain (E=3); less sustained than wind (E=4-5). Wave period 5-15 seconds creates perceptible rhythm.

tidal_context.puget_sound_pattern: Mixed semidiurnal: 2 highs, 2 lows per day, unequal heights
tidal_context.deception_pass_current_knots: 8-9 at maximum ebb/flood -- among strongest in Puget Sound

primary_locations: Deception Pass State Park (~4 million visitors/year, most-visited WA state park)
San Juan Islands (172 named islands, complex tidal flows, extensive cobble shoreline)
Strait of Juan de Fuca shorelines

cultural_significance.indigenous: Coast Salish, Lummi, Samish and other Puget Sound peoples. Clam harvesting (gravel substrate = butter clams, littleneck clams, geoduck). Travel routes along shoreline. Clamshell middens throughout San Juans and northern Sound -- thousands of years of continuous activity.
cultural_significance.archaeological: Clamshell middens demonstrating continuous human use over millennia
cultural_significance.recreational: Deception Pass most-visited WA state park. Cobble beach as defining PNW recreational coastal experience.
cultural_significance.aesthetic: Grey cobble under grey sky -- the PNW coastal aesthetic: stony, cool, austere, precisely itself

theory_nodes.concept: METEOROLOGICAL/HYDROLOGICAL ARC COMPLETE (249-252). Wind through Sitka Spruce (249, Meteorology, E=4) → Wind through Old-Growth Fir (250, Meteorology, E=5) → Pacific Rain on Water (251, Meteorology, E=3) → Tidal Surge on Cobble (252, Hydrology, E=4). Arc transitions from meteorological to hydrological. Water in atmosphere (rain) → water on coast (tidal surge). Arc closes with stone.
theory_nodes.description: 249-252 arc complete; meteorological to hydrological transition.

theory_nodes.concept: Lithophone: the cobble beach is a natural percussion instrument. Wave energy = player; cobbles = instrument. PNW's volcanic/metamorphic geology produces cobble rather than sand, making the coast a lithophonic landscape. Sandy beaches absorb wave energy silently; cobble beaches resonate. The geology makes the sound possible.
theory_nodes.description: Lithophonic coast: geology as acoustic instrument design.

theory_nodes.concept: Backwash clatter as signature PNW cobble sound. When retreating wave drags cobbles downslope against incoming wave resistance, thousands of stones slide against each other producing sustained, rhythmic, complex percussion -- the most distinctive and memorable sound of the PNW cobble beach. Deception Pass: 8-9 knot currents, cobble beaches on both Sound and Rosario sides. Most-visited WA state park.
theory_nodes.description: Backwash clatter as defining cobble sound.

cross_references.relationship: Meteorological to hydrological transition. Rain (water from atmosphere on open water, E=3) → tidal surge (water from ocean on stone coast, E=4). Energy rises in final arc entry.

First abiotic entry (245) and final arc entry (252) both involve water moving stone. River current → tidal surge. The abiotic water-stone interaction documented at both ends of the abiotic phase opening arc.

s36_pair.pairing_note: E=7 vs E=4. Noise rock abrasion vs. stone percussion. Both: force against hard material (guitar pick/string; wave/stone). Both produce complex, irregular, unpitched percussion. Intentional vs. accidental: No-Anchor chooses their sounds deliberately; the tide imposes through pure physics.
